answers.question: List of scenarios
answers.evidence: International Scenario Archetype: NGFS – Orderly / RCP 1.9 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: NZE | NGFS – Disorderly / RCP 2.6 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: SDS | NGFS – Hot House / RCP 8.5 / SSP5: Fossil Fuel Development / CCC: Current Policy Reference / IEA: STEPS
answers.notes: CMC developed three customised scenarios (Orderly Transition, Disorderly Transition, Hot House) mapped to multiple international scenario archetypes from NGFS, RCP, SSP, CCC, and IEA frameworks.

answers.question: Global temperature outcome by 2100
answers.answer: Three temperature outcomes are described: 1.4°C (Orderly Transition), 1.6°C (Disorderly Transition), and >3.0°C (Hot House). No explicit reference to 2100 is made; these are described as "modelled global temperature increase".
answers.evidence: Modelled global temperature increase: 1.4°C [Scenario 1 Orderly Transition] | 1.6°C [Scenario 2 Disorderly Transition] | >3.0°C [Scenario 3 Hot House]
answers.location_hint: Section 2, Scenario Analysis Process table, p.2
answers.notes: The document does not explicitly state these are by 2100, but they are presented as modelled global temperature increases associated with each scenario pathway.

answers.question: Scenario provider
answers.answer: Multiple scenario providers are explicitly named: NGFS (Network for Greening the Financial System), CCC (Climate Change Commission), and IEA (International Energy Agency). RCP and SSP frameworks are also referenced (implicitly from IPCC).
answers.evidence: International Scenario Archetype: NGFS – Orderly / RCP 1.9 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: NZE | NGFS – Disorderly / RCP 2.6 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: SDS | NGFS – Hot House / RCP 8.5 / SSP5: Fossil Fuel Development / CCC: Current Policy Reference / IEA: STEPS
answers.location_hint: Section 2, Scenario Analysis Process table, p.2
answers.notes: NGFS, CCC (New Zealand Climate Change Commission), and IEA are explicitly named. RCP and SSP are standard IPCC frameworks but IPCC is not explicitly named as a provider in the document.

answers.question: Pick-and-mix of SSPs and RCPs
answers.evidence: NGFS – Orderly / RCP 1.9 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: NZE | NGFS – Disorderly / RCP 2.6 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: SDS | NGFS – Hot House / RCP 8.5 / SSP5: Fossil Fuel Development / CCC: Current Policy Reference / IEA: STEPS
answers.location_hint: Section 2, Scenario Analysis Process table, p.2
answers.notes: The document explicitly lists both RCP identifiers (RCP 1.9, RCP 2.6, RCP 8.5) and SSP identifiers (SSP1: Sustainability, SSP5: Fossil Fuel Development) as archetypes for the same scenarios. RCP 1.9 is not a standard IPCC scenario (the closest is RCP 2.6/SSP1-1.9), suggesting mixing of scenario families.

answers.question: Customised scenarios
answers.evidence: The Company engaged an external consultant to assist the Management team to establish customised scenario narratives for CMC in accordance with NZCS1 and NZCS3. These were then presented to the Board for their consideration and approval.
answers.notes: The document explicitly uses the word "customised" to describe the scenario narratives developed for CMC. The scenarios were adapted for the automotive/transport industry context in New Zealand, with parameters such as national vehicle fleet composition and consumer sentiment tailored to CMC's business.

answers.question: Co-produced climate scenarios
answers.answer: Yes — partial evidence. Management participated in the Transport Sector Climate Scenarios Working Group, which aimed to develop an integrated set of scenarios across the transport sector. However, the CMC scenarios themselves were developed with an external consultant rather than through a fully participatory process.
answers.evidence: During 2023-24, Management participated in the Transport Sector Climate Scenarios Working Group whose aim was to develop an integrated set of scenarios across the transport sector. The CMC scenario analysis process maintained consistency with the transport sector by using the same time horizons as the Working Group. Although the outputs of the Working Group were not available when CMC undertook its customised analysis, the Company's scenario frameworks are similar to those used by the Working Group.
answers.notes: The document describes participation in a sector-wide Working Group, which implies collaborative/participatory development at the sector level. However, CMC's own scenarios were developed with an external consultant and presented to the Board, not through a fully co-designed process. No keywords like "co-creat", "deliberat", or "co-design" are used explicitly.

answers.question: Baseline scenarios
answers.answer: No explicit baseline or reference scenario is described against which the climate scenarios are compared. The "CCC: Current Policy Reference" is listed as an archetype for Scenario 3 (Hot House), but it is not framed as a standalone baseline for comparison purposes.
answers.evidence: International Scenario Archetype [for Scenario 3 Hot House]: NGFS – Hot House / RCP 8.5 / SSP5: Fossil Fuel Development / CCC: Current Policy Reference / IEA: STEPS
answers.location_hint: Section 2, Scenario Analysis Process table, p.2
answers.notes: The "CCC: Current Policy Reference" could be interpreted as a baseline/reference scenario, but the document does not explicitly frame any scenario as a baseline against which others are compared.

answers.question: Model used
answers.notes: No specific climate models, integrated assessment models (IAMs), or macroeconomic models are named in the document. The document references scenario frameworks (NGFS, RCP, SSP, CCC, IEA) but does not name any underlying models such as CLIMADA, REMIND, NiGEM, etc.

answers.question: Damage from acute physical risk considered
answers.answer: Yes — the document mentions damage and costs from extreme/worsening weather events as a physical risk, but does not provide quantified loss estimates.
answers.evidence: Property and vehicle stocks (physical): Assets can be physically impacted by climate change. This is likely to incur costs to prevent or repair damage. Worsening weather events, or chronic impacts for example sea level change, have the potential to increase the cost of asset ownership or decrease the value of property. [...] New Zealand supply chains can be disrupted due to severe weather events, or by the repairs or strengthening work associated with storm damage or mitigation/adaption programmes.
answers.location_hint: Section 3, Risks and Opportunities table, p.3–4
answers.notes: The document qualitatively describes damage/costs from acute physical events (worsening weather events, storm damage, supply chain disruptions from physical events) but does not provide quantified damage or loss estimates. It is qualitative rather than quantitative scenario analysis of acute physical risk.

answers.question: Non-temperature variables in scenarios
answer: National vehicle fleet composition (Low Emission Vehicles)
Severe weather events / worsening weather events
answers.evidence: Scenario Framework and Parameters: Global policy reaction | Regional policy variation | Speed of technological change | Consumer sentiment / behaviour change | Physical risks severity | Transition risks severity | National vehicle fleet composition [...] Worsening weather events, or chronic impacts for example sea level change, have the potential to increase the cost of asset ownership or decrease the value of property.
answers.location_hint: Section 2, Scenario Analysis Process table, p.2; Section 3, Risks and Opportunities, p.3
answers.notes: These variables are used to characterise the scenarios. Sea level change and severe weather events are mentioned in the risk section rather than the scenario table itself.

answers.question: Scale of scenarios
answers.evidence: Modelled global temperature increase: 1.4°C | 1.6°C | >3.0°C [...] International Scenario Archetype: NGFS – Orderly / RCP 1.9 / SSP1: Sustainability / CCC: Tailwinds / IEA: NZE
answers.location_hint: Section 2, Scenario Analysis Process table, p.2
answers.notes: The scenarios are based on global archetypes (NGFS, RCP, SSP, IEA) and reference "modelled global temperature increase." There is no mention of downscaling to regional or local level. The document does reference New Zealand-specific policy and fleet composition as scenario parameters, but these appear to be narrative adaptations rather than downscaled climate modelling.

answers.question: Time frames
answers.answer: Three time horizons are used: short (2024–2030), medium (2030–2040), and long (2040–2050). Rationale is provided: short and medium align with CMC's existing strategic planning focused on automotive product and economic cycles; the longer term is relevant to the CMC property portfolio and organisational approach.
answers.evidence: Narratives (hypothetical pathways of plausible actions) were mapped out for each scenario framework using three time horizons: short (2024-2030), medium (2030-2040) and long (2040-2050). The short and medium time horizons align with existing CMC strategic planning which focus on automotive product and economic cycles and the longer term is relevant to the CMC property portfolio and organisational approach.
answers.notes: Justification is explicitly provided for all three time horizons.

answers.question: Justification for scenario/model choice
answers.answer: Yes — partial justification is provided. The document states that scenarios were developed in accordance with NZCS1 and NZCS3 requirements, and that consistency with the Transport Sector Climate Scenarios Working Group was maintained. No explicit justification is given for the choice of specific international archetypes (NGFS, RCP, SSP, IEA).
answers.evidence: The Company engaged an external consultant to assist the Management team to establish customised scenario narratives for CMC in accordance with NZCS1 and NZCS3. [...] During 2023-24, Management participated in the Transport Sector Climate Scenarios Working Group whose aim was to develop an integrated set of scenarios across the transport sector. The CMC scenario analysis process maintained consistency with the transport sector by using the same time horizons as the Working Group. Although the outputs of the Working Group were not available when CMC undertook its customised analysis, the Company's scenario frameworks are similar to those used by the Working Group.
answers.notes: The justification given is regulatory compliance (NZCS1/NZCS3) and alignment with the transport sector working group. No explicit justification is provided for why specific international scenario archetypes (NGFS, RCP, SSP, IEA) were selected over alternatives.
